Mathematical Foundations

This chapter derives the core mathematics behind linGAM: B-spline bases, penalised least squares, the SVD data-augmentation solver, effective degrees of freedom, and Generalised Cross Validation (GCV).

B-spline bases

A B-spline of order \(k\) (polynomial degree \(k-1\)) is built from \(K\) basis functions via the De Boor recursion. Given data \(x \in [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]\), we first map to \([0, 1]\):

\[z = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}.\]

Internal knots are placed uniformly:

\[\tau_0 = 0, \; \tau_1, \dots, \tau_{K-k+1} = 1.\]

The augmented knot sequence extends \(k\) knots beyond each boundary.

De Boor recursion. Start with order-1 (piecewise constant) bases:

\[B_{i,1}(z) = \mathbf{1}_{[\tau_i, \tau_{i+1})}(z).\]

For orders \(m = 2, \dots, k\):

\[B_{i,m}(z) = \frac{z - \tau_i}{\tau_{i+m-1} - \tau_i} \, B_{i,m-1}(z) + \frac{\tau_{i+m} - z}{\tau_{i+m} - \tau_{i+1}} \, B_{i+1,m-1}(z).\]

The result is an \(n \times K\) design matrix \(B\) with the property that each row sums to 1 and each column has compact support (localised).

Linear extrapolation. Outside \([0,1]\), B-splines shoot to zero or oscillate wildly. linGAM replaces the B-spline values with a linear extension using the boundary gradient:

\[f(z) \approx f(0) + f'(0) \, z \quad (z < 0), \qquad f(z) \approx f(1) + f'(1) \, (z-1) \quad (z > 1).\]

The derivative \(f'(0)\) is computed analytically from the \((k-1)\)-order basis saved during recursion.

Periodic basis. When constraint='periodic', the basis is wrapped so that the first \(k-1\) columns are added to the last \(k-1\) columns. This enforces \(f(0) = f(1)\) and reduces the coefficient count from \(K\) to \(K - k + 1\).

Penalised least squares

The model is

\[\mathbf{y} = X \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon},\]

where \(X\) stacks all term design matrices (plus an intercept column). We minimise the penalised sum of squares:

\[S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \|\mathbf{y} - X \boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top P \boldsymbol{\beta}.\]

The penalty \(P\) is block-diagonal, with one block per term. The intercept block is zero (unpenalised).

Second-difference penalty (smoothness). For a spline with \(K\) coefficients:

\[\begin{split}D_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2 & 1 & & \\ & 1 & -2 & 1 & \\ & & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots \\ \end{pmatrix}_{(K-2) \times K}, \qquad P = \lambda \, D_2^\top D_2.\end{split}\]

This penalises curvature: neighbouring coefficients that differ wildly incur a large cost.

First-difference penalty (monotonicity). For constraint='mono_inc' or 'mono_dec':

\[\begin{split}D_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 & & \\ & -1 & 1 & \\ & & \ddots & \ddots \end{pmatrix}_{(K-1) \times K}, \qquad P = \lambda \, D_1^\top D_1.\end{split}\]

Minimising \(\beta^\top D_1^\top D_1 \beta = \sum_i (\beta_{i+1} - \beta_i)^2\) forces adjacent coefficients to be similar, which encourages a monotonic shape. Combined with the natural ordering of B-spline basis functions, this produces an increasing (or decreasing) smooth.

Circular second-difference (periodicity). For constraint='periodic':

\[\begin{split} P_{ij} = \lambda \times \begin{cases} 4 & i = j \\ -2 & j = (i \pm 1) \bmod K_{\text{eff}} \\ 1 & j = (i \pm 2) \bmod K_{\text{eff}} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\end{split}\]

This wraps the second-difference operator, connecting the first and last coefficients so the curve is closed.

Ridge penalty. For factor and linear terms:

\[P = \lambda I_K.\]

SVD data-augmentation solver

The normal equations are \((X^\top X + P)\beta = X^\top y\). Forming \(X^\top X\) squares the condition number and amplifies numerical instability. linGAM uses a data-augmentation trick instead.

  1. Cholesky of the penalty. Factor \(P + \varepsilon I = E^\top E\) where \(\varepsilon = \sqrt{\text{machine epsilon}}\) is a tiny ridge that guarantees positive definiteness even when \(\lambda = 0\).

  2. QR of the design matrix. \(X = QR\) where \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(R\) is upper-triangular. Because \(Q\) preserves norms, working with \(R\) (size \(m \times m\)) instead of \(X\) (size \(n \times m\)) is numerically safe and cheaper when \(n \gg m\).

  3. SVD of the stacked matrix. Form \([R; E]\) and decompose:

    \[[R; E] = U \, \text{diag}(d) \, V^\top.\]

    This is equivalent to solving the augmented least-squares system

    \[\begin{split}\begin{pmatrix} X \\ E \end{pmatrix} \beta = \begin{pmatrix} y \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\end{split}\]

    but never forms \(X^\top X\) explicitly.

  4. Solution operator. Truncate to the first \(\min(m,n)\) singular values and compute:

    \[B_{\text{solve}} = V \, \text{diag}(d^{-1}) \, U_1^\top Q^\top, \qquad \hat\beta = B_{\text{solve}} \, y.\]

    Saving \(B_{\text{solve}}\) lets us compute covariance and effective degrees of freedom later without re-fitting.

Why this works. The stacked SVD is the most numerically stable way to solve a penalised linear system. Tiny singular values are handled gracefully (by division, not inversion of a near-singular matrix), and the condition number of \([R; E]\) is the square root of the condition number of \(X^\top X + P\).

Effective degrees of freedom (EDF)

In ordinary linear regression, the degrees of freedom equal the number of parameters. In a penalised spline, the penalty “shrinks” coefficients, so each one uses less than one full degree of freedom.

The EDF is the trace of the influence (hat) matrix:

\[\text{EDF} = \text{tr}(H), \qquad H = X (X^\top X + P)^{-1} X^\top.\]

Using the SVD augmentation, this simplifies to

\[\text{EDF} = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(m,n)} \sigma_i(U_1)^2 = \|U_1\|_F^2,\]

where \(U_1\) is the top-left \(\min(m,n) \times \min(m,n)\) block of \(U\). This is computed in a single NumPy call with no extra matrix inversions.

Per-coefficient EDF tells us how much each individual parameter contributes:

\[\text{EDF}_j = \sum_i U_{1,ij}^2.\]

Generalised Cross Validation (GCV)

Leave-one-out cross validation is expensive: fit the model \(n\) times, dropping one point each time. GCV is a clever closed-form approximation:

\[\text{GCV} = \frac{n \cdot \text{RSS}}{(n - \gamma \cdot \text{EDF})^2},\]

where

  • \(n\) = number of observations,

  • \(\text{RSS} = \sum_i (y_i - \hat y_i)^2\) = residual sum of squares,

  • \(\text{EDF}\) = effective degrees of freedom,

  • \(\gamma\) = complexity penalty multiplier (default 1.4).

The denominator penalises model complexity. When \(\text{EDF}\) grows, the denominator shrinks and GCV explodes — this prevents overfitting. Setting \(\gamma > 1\) (e.g. 1.4) is analogous to AIC/BIC corrections that prefer slightly simpler models.

Why GCV and not plain CV? GCV requires only a single fit. It is asymptotically equivalent to leave-one-out CV but computes in \(O(1)\) extra time after the fit.

Fast QR+Cholesky grid search

Grid search evaluates every combination of \((K, \lambda)\) candidates. A naïve implementation rebuilds \(X\) and solves from scratch for each combination. linGAM’s fast path avoids redundant work.

Phase 1 — Precompute per :math:`K` (threaded). For each candidate basis count \(K\):

  1. Build \(X_K\) and compute its QR decomposition: \(X_K = Q_K R_K\).

  2. Form \(R_K^\top R_K\) and \(R_K^\top Q_K^\top y\). These are independent of :math:`lambda`.

  3. Precompute the unscaled penalty basis \(S = D^\top D\) (also independent of \(\lambda\)).

All of this is done once per \(K\) and cached.

Phase 2 — Evaluate per :math:`(K, lambda)` (threaded). For each combination:

  1. Assemble the penalty: \(P = \lambda S\).

  2. Build \(B = R_K^\top R_K + P\).

  3. Cholesky solve: \(\hat\beta = B^{-1} (R_K^\top Q_K^\top y)\).

  4. Compute EDF via \(\text{tr}(B^{-1} R_K^\top R_K)\).

  5. Compute RSS and GCV.

Cholesky costs \(O(m^3/3)\) versus \(O(m^3)\) for SVD, and both phases are fully parallel via concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor.

Precomputed penalty bases. For multi-term models, each term’s penalty is built from unscaled base matrices (e.g. \(D_2^\top D_2\) at \(\lambda = 1\)). During Phase 2, assembly is just element-wise scaling: \(P_{ij} += \lambda_k \cdot S_{ij}\). No matrix is rebuilt from scratch.

Tensor-product interactions

A tensor term te(f1, f2, ..., fd) models interactions without assuming additivity. The design matrix is the Khatri-Rao (column-wise Kronecker) product of marginal B-spline bases:

\[B_{\text{tensor}} = B_1 \odot B_2 \odot \dots \odot B_d,\]

where \(\odot\) denotes column-wise product. If \(B_i\) is \(n \times K_i\), the result is \(n \times (K_1 K_2 \dots K_d)\).

The penalty is the Kronecker sum of marginal penalties:

\[P_{\text{tensor}} = \lambda_1 (P_1 \otimes I_{K_2} \otimes \dots \otimes I_{K_d}) + \lambda_2 (I_{K_1} \otimes P_2 \otimes \dots \otimes I_{K_d}) + \dots + \lambda_d (I_{K_1} \otimes \dots \otimes I_{K_{d-1}} \otimes P_d).\]

Each direction can have its own smoothing parameter \(\lambda_i\).

For a 2-D tensor te(0, 1) with \(K_1 = K_2 = 6\), the design matrix has \(n \times 36\) columns and the penalty is \(36 \times 36\).

Huber IRLS (robust fitting)

Ordinary least squares is sensitive to outliers. Robust fitting replaces the squared loss with the Huber loss:

\[\begin{split}\rho(r) = \begin{cases} \tfrac12 r^2 & |r| \le c \\ c(|r| - \tfrac12 c) & |r| > c \end{cases}\end{split}\]

where \(c = 1.345 \, \hat\sigma\) and \(\hat\sigma = \text{MAD} / 0.6745\). The constant 1.345 gives ~95% asymptotic efficiency under Gaussian errors.

The IRLS algorithm iterates:

  1. Compute residuals \(r = y - X\hat\beta\).

  2. Compute weights \(w_i = \min\bigl(1, c / |r_i|\bigr)\).

  3. Solve the weighted penalised system with weights \(\sqrt{w_i}\).

  4. Repeat until convergence (default 15 iterations for fit, 10 for grid search).

This is equivalent to M-estimation with the Huber ψ-function and is implemented by weighting rows of \(X\) and \(y\) by \(\sqrt{w}\) before calling the standard PIRLS solver.

Confidence and prediction intervals

The variance of the fitted mean at a new point \(x^*\) is:

\[\text{Var}\bigl(\hat y(x^*)\bigr) = x^{*\top} \, \text{Cov}(\hat\beta) \, x^*.\]

The covariance is:

\[\text{Cov}(\hat\beta) = B_{\text{solve}} B_{\text{solve}}^\top \, \hat\sigma^2, \qquad \hat\sigma^2 = \frac{\text{RSS}}{n - \text{EDF}}.\]

Confidence intervals (for the mean response) use:

\[\hat y(x^*) \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, n-\text{EDF}} \, \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat y(x^*))}.\]

Prediction intervals (for a new observation) add the observation noise:

\[\hat y(x^*) \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, n-\text{EDF}} \, \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat y(x^*)) + \hat\sigma^2}.\]

The t-distribution is used instead of the normal because \(\sigma\) is estimated from data. Degrees of freedom are \(n - \text{EDF}\) because the penalty has already “consumed” EDF degrees of freedom.
